content: 101. Statement of Police Assistant Constable Ganesh Khadka, serving at Police Division Koteshwar:

My name is Ganesh Khadka. I am currently 34 years old. My grandfather's name is Hari Bahadur Khadka and my father's name is Chakra Bahadur Khadka. My district is Dolakha, Gaurishankar Rural Municipality, Ward No. 5. I currently live with my family in a rented room in Sanepа, Lalitpur. I joined the police service on Chaitra 25, 2069, and have been serving at Police Division Koteshwar for the past six months. During demonstrations by the Gen-Z youth generation in Kathmandu and various parts of the country on Bhadra 23 and 24, 2082, I was deployed on duty in the ward area of Police Division Koteshwar. On Bhadra 23, 2082, I was deployed on duty at Police Division Koteshwar, Kathmandu under the command of Police Sub-Inspector Indra Bahadur Basnet for office security. On Bhadra 24, under the command of Police Sub-Inspector Indra Bahadur Basnet for the security of Koteshwar Chowk and Police Division Koteshwar  at approximately 11:00 AM, as agitators began attacking police from all four sides with stones, bricks, petrol bombs, and batons, we fell back and entered the office compound. After that, as they began attempting to set fire to the office, while requesting the crowd to move some distance away and coming outside to persuade them, an agitated group attacked me along with others from all four sides with stones, bricks, khukuris, and spears  causing the body protector, helmet, and shield I was wearing to break. When the crowd attacked to the point where resistance and self-defense became impossible, all police personnel began fleeing in all directions to save their lives. I too fled and entered the Bhrikuti Bhawan next to Police Division, saving my life. I did not know where the others went  inside Bhrikuti Bhawan I had approximately 15–16 colleagues, some of whom were injured while others were unharmed. My current condition is normal. I do not recognize any of the people who were attacking at that time. The statement I have given to the best of my knowledge is true and accurate.
102. Statement of Police Assistant Constable Rudra Prasad Gautam, serving at Police Division Koteshwar:

During demonstrations by the Gen-Z youth generation in Kathmandu and various parts of the country on Bhadra 23 and 24, 2082, I was deployed as the duty officer's assistant at Police Division Koteshwar. On Bhadra 23, I was deployed on duty at Police Division Koteshwar, Kathmandu under the command of Police Sub-Inspector Indra Bahadur Basnet for office security. On Bhadra 24, deployed as duty officer's assistant under the command of Police Assistant Sub-Inspector Hareram Kumal  at approximately 10:30 AM, when a group of 400–500 came and attempted to attack the office and us, locals from Koteshwar Chowk joined hands and persuaded the crowd that they should not commit arson at the police office or beat police, and sent them toward Tinkune Baneswhar. At around 11:00 AM, a mob coming from the Koteshwar overpass went to the Koteshwar Traffic Office, vandalized it, set it on fire, and shortly afterward that mob came to the gate of Police Division Koteshwar, threw stones, and hurled petrol bombs from outside, which we extinguished twice. At approximately 12:30, a mob of approximately 3,000–4,000 came to the gate outside Police Division Koteshwar, threw stones, threatened from outside  'Either come out of the office or we will burn you alive inside'  and began attacking. Being forced to flee, I and 5–6 colleagues fled along the sidewalk on the left side of our office and entered a local's home, going up 4–5 floors and locking ourselves inside a room from within. After that mob burned the office and came to where we were staying, they broke the door from outside and entered. As soon as they entered, I broke the glass window of that room and went outside, jumped from that building to another building, landing in a flower garden where I stayed. Within 10–15 minutes of having escaped from that room, a group of 25–30 people found me and said 'this one must die'  they grabbed me, dragged me by the feet down from the roof of that building, struck my head with bricks, batons, and kicks on the way down, after which I became semi-conscious. I was thrown from the roof of that building to the edge of the sidewalk below. I vaguely remember some people saying 'kill this one,' others saying 'this one is dead, leave him,' and some saying 'let's throw him in the hospital.' After being beaten on the sidewalk I fell into unconsciousness, and a local woman said 'this is the brother from Koteshwar, I know him'  that woman sent me to KMC Hospital in Sinamangal. When I regained consciousness on the 24th, I was in a bed at Kathmandu Medical College in Sinamangal. My current condition is normal. I do not recognize any of the people who were attacking at that time.
103. Statement of Police Assistant Constable Yogendra Bahadur Chand, serving at Police Circle Kalimati:

On Bhadra 23, 2082, I was deployed on duty at the Crime Investigation Branch inside the office. No incident occurred within my responsibility that day and duty was completed normally. From 7:00 AM on Bhadra 24, 2082, I was again on duty at that same branch. At approximately 8:00 AM that morning, information came through the police control room communication set that some agitators were burning tires and demonstrating at Kalankhi Chowk, Ward No. 14, Kathmandu Metropolitan City, Kathmandu district. At that time, a police team under Police Inspector Bijayraj Timalsina of this same office went to that location, while police personnel at the office including myself were deployed at the main gate of Police Circle Kalimati for office security. Some time later, DSP Madhu Kumar Thapa  who had been injured in the Gen-Z movement at Baneswhar on the 23rd and was staying at this office while receiving treatment  took additional medication and a pain-reducing injection and immediately went with an additional team toward Kalankhi Chowk despite being injured. At approximately 10:00 AM that morning, approximately 100 demonstrators raising slogans passed through the road in front of this Police Circle toward Kalimati Chowk. Approximately 30 minutes later, approximately 4,000–5,000 demonstrators came carrying weapons to the office gate and raised slogans: 'Police shot and killed people in Baneswhar on the 23rd, these people must also be killed, burn them alive.' Kathmandu Valley Police Control Room at Bhadrakali broadcast instructions through communication sets not to use much force, not to fire live rounds, and to act with restraint to prevent further human casualties  so we sat defensively, repeatedly making public address announcements urging the crowd to remain calm, but nobody listened and continuous attacks on police began. The demonstrators surrounded all four sides of this Police Circle, throwing stones, bricks, and petrol bombs. DSP Madhu Kumar Thapa and his police team arrived at this office, and a police team under 2 Police Inspectors deployed from Municipal Police Office Bhrikuti Mandap also arrived. After that, we fired tear gas and dispersed the crowd, entered the circle, and removed 300–400 demonstrators who were inside. After thousands of demonstrators coming from Kalankhi direction and demonstrators from Balkhu direction and demonstrators in Kalimati all met, the situation went beyond control. Smoke from fires on the roads and at police offices rose in all directions. Demonstrators from Balkhu direction arrived on motorcycles carrying shotguns, 303 rifles, and SLRs; some demonstrators were wearing police uniforms. When a strong team from Police Circle Kalimati and municipal police tried to stop demonstrators at the gate, as they could not enter through the gate, they went into buildings on the right, left, and rear and threw petrol bombs from rooftops. This caused the rooms on the front road-facing side of the Police Circle to catch fire. The traffic building at the rear also caught fire, and vans of traffic police parked on the side, other vehicles, and the traffic police van at this office gate also caught fire. As armed demonstrators entered through the main gate of the circle and tried to seize a shotgun and ammunition from police and take over other weapons, repeated public address announcements were made by police and some dummy rounds were fired aerially for self-defense. As demonstrators on motorcycles and minivans from Balaju and Swayambhu direction arrived shouting that all police must be killed and tried to enter the circle, when the circle started burning and even public address announcements were not heeded, after aerial fire, demonstrators retreated somewhat  seizing that opportunity, detainees in this circle's custody were released temporarily for life preservation purposes per the office chief's orders with notification to the supervisory office, telling them to appear later. After Kathmandu Valley Police Control at Bhadrakali informed that all personnel should leave the office and take weapons, communication sets, and vehicles to a safe location to prevent further human casualties, all weapons and communication sets were loaded into a police van and personnel went via Kalimati Chowk, Teku, Shahid Gate to Bhadrakali Range. My police team along with others was also pelted with stones on the way. Despite requesting additional support repeatedly, we received information that additional support from other security agencies could not come and to keep ourselves safe. Later, as thousands of demonstrators came to Bhadrakali Range carrying weapons to attack, after public address announcements were not heeded, some rounds of tear gas and dummy round aerial fire were used, which prevented arson and looting at Bhadrakali Range. We acted per orders of the commander deployed for security at the District Police Range. There were dozens of attempts to attack the District Police Range. At approximately 19:30, as demonstrators coming on more than 100 motorcycles and in Armed Police Force trucks tried to ram a truck into the gate to break it, we stopped them with tear gas and baton charges. All kinds of people were involved in the attacks that day  mostly drug users, those working at transportation counters in the Kalankhi-Balkhu area, laborers, squatter settlement residents, scrap workers of Indian origin, and detainees who had escaped from police custody  I estimate. I cannot say for certain who exactly joined together. After our police team left the circle, arson and looting occurred at Kalimati Circle, completely damaging all materials, furniture, and personal vehicles of police personnel in all branches. All vehicles in this office compound were completely damaged by arson. A shotgun and ammunition were looted from this office  the shotgun has since been recovered but the ammunition has not yet been found. In that incident, 60–65 police personnel from this office were injured, and some demonstrators were also injured from baton charges. I don't have the exact number of those. Police personnel were injured from attacks and beatings by demonstrators, while demonstrators were injured from police baton charges and in the course of fleeing. As per orders from the supervisory office, we used minimum force and saved weapons, communication sets, and police vans at risk to our own lives. In that incident, personal vehicles and all belongings of police personnel including myself were burned and destroyed.
104. Statement of Police Constable Ashok Kumar Awasthi, serving at Police Circle Kirtipur:

On Bhadra 23, 2082, I was doing regular work at the Crime Investigation Branch of my own office. Even while working at the branch, I was aware of the immediate situation of the movement. For that reason, I was troubled thinking about the next day's events and the state of peace, security, and order in the country. Despite various thoughts disturbing my mind until late that night, while thinking about the next day's security, I fell asleep without realizing it. Getting up early the next morning, completing my daily routine, and understanding the current situation, as we were about to go to the mess for food, everyone suddenly took their equipment and under orders from Commander DSP Raj Babu Maharjan  at approximately 11:00 AM, a group of approximately 1,000–1,200 demonstrators came and attempted to stone-throw at the office, whereupon Commander DSP Raj Babu Maharjan persuaded the group of agitators and sent them away. The situation was normal for a while, but information was also received that arson and vandalism had occurred at various locations in Kirtipur. At approximately 12:00 noon, a group of 3,000–4,000 agitators again came, entered the office, and attempted stone-throwing, arson, and looting. Under Commander DSP Raj Babu Maharjan's orders, persuasion was attempted and graduated crowd control measures were adopted as much as possible to the point where shooting would have been required, but as per supervisory office orders and considering the immediate situation of minimizing human casualties  since firing would cause excessive human casualties  we were deployed to protect the office and its materials to the extent possible. At that point, a group of aggressive, lawless anarchists entered the office compound from all four sides and completely damaged the office and police barracks through vandalism, looting, and arson. While still requesting them not to commit arson, we took our weapons and went to a safe location. The entire day passed in a state of fear and terror. Even in such circumstances, due to the skilled leadership and situational assessment ability of Commander DSP Raj Babu Maharjan, no human casualties occurred, and as a result, the days following were easier for daily police operations and there was a positive effect on police morale. This is true and accurate.
105. Statement of Police Constable Manoj Gyawali, serving at Parliament Building Security Base, Baneswhar:

High Point Posts are maintained on all four sides of the Federal Parliament Building, and personnel are deployed at those posts around the clock on rotating duty with INSAS rifles. That area is a restricted zone where activities such as sit-ins and demonstrations are prohibited. On days when Parliament sessions are in session at the Federal Parliament Building, Nepal Police and Armed Police Force also come for additional security. On Bhadra 23, 2082, my duty was at High Post No. 14 on the Baneswhar Chowk side of the Federal Parliament Building from 10:00 AM to 2:00 PM with an INSAS rifle and 36 rounds of ammunition. As the National Assembly session was also being held that day, additional security from Nepal Police and Armed Police Force had also arrived. I was on my own duty. At approximately 11:00 or 11:30, thousands of demonstrators from all four sides, growing agitated, surrounded the Parliament Building and attempted to break walls and doors and enter the Parliament Building. Immediately, additional support from Nepal Police and Armed Police Force arrived and began persuading the demonstrators. Demonstrators did not listen to police and became more anarchic, breaking and vandalizing the Parliament Building and attempting to enter. In the course of this, they came near my post and chanting 'attack attack' began throwing stones at me. After stone-throwing began, I came out from my post with my weapon and fell back. Demonstrators began vandalizing the Parliament Building entrance gate and police posts. Sounds of gas gun and bullet fire from outside began to be heard. I did not fire my weapon. In the evening, the crowd went to their respective destinations and things calmed down. After it calmed down, I learned through Facebook and various social media that demonstrators had been injured and had died. That evening, a Nepali Army team also came and began providing security for the Federal Parliament Building. On the following day, the 24th, from the morning, Nepal Army, Armed Police Force, Nepal Police, and police personnel from the Federal Parliament Building Security Base Baneswhar were all providing security for the Federal Parliament Building. That day I had no weapon with me  I was in crowd control attire with a baton. That day too, the Gen-Z generation was conducting an all-around movement. A large group of demonstrators had surrounded the Parliament Building from all four sides and was demonstrating. While demonstrators were demonstrating and security police were continuing to persuade them, at approximately 13:00, an uncontrollable large number gathered around the Parliament Building and targeted it with stone-throwing, slingshots, and petrol bombs and other flammable substances  injuring many police  broke through the Parliament Building compound walls using pickaxes and similar tools, and entered the Parliament Building compound brandishing khukuris while creating commotion, and set fire to the Parliament Building. After that, we secured our weapons and ammunition.
106. Statement of Police Constable Jitendra Mahat, serving at Police Circle Balaju:

On Bhadra 23, 2082, from 2:00 PM I was deployed as additional support duty at Shital Niwas gate outside the Presidential Palace under the command of Police Circle Balaju Chief DSP Gyan Bahadur Bista. The situation was normal at my deployed location that day, but in the evening, Gen-Z movement people on motorcycles were spitting at police and engaging in verbal abuse. That day, Gen-Z group members were demonstrating and moving at Maitighar Mandala area demanding national interest, good governance, and corruption control, and in the evening I learned through social media that shots had been fired as they tried to vandalize and set fire to the Parliament Building, resulting in the death of some demonstrators. The next day as well, I was deployed on vehicle duty under DSP Gyan Bahadur Bista's command. We were on duty in the ward area of Police Circle Balaju. First arriving at Kharibot, I saw many people gathered there. None of the demonstrators were in school uniforms. Those gathered came to Banasthali Chowk and were burning tires and demonstrating. The crowd was extremely large. The crowd began throwing stones and setting fire to the Banasthali Police Post. As we had few personnel, nothing other than persuasion could be done. After that, upon hearing rumors that the crowd might set fire to the President's private residence in Bohora, our team went toward the President's private residence. Demonstrators came toward the President's private residence carrying bamboo batons, bottles, stones, and petrol bombs. We had no personnel other than the vehicle team. When we tried to persuade the demonstrators, the crowd became more aggressive and anarchic. After persuasion did not work, we tried to disperse the crowd with tear gas and rubber bullets. The crowd did not comply. As the crowd was extremely large, they came back and attacked police, and began arson, looting, and vandalism at the President's private residence. Using firearms would likely have caused large numbers of demonstrators to be hit. After that, when orders came through the communication set to protect ourselves and the office, we walked back toward the office with the help of locals. The DSP had already sent the vehicle to the office at the beginning fearing it might be vandalized. Upon arriving at the office, no demonstrators were around Balaju Circle in school uniforms. Approximately 2,000–3,000 demonstrators gathered at the office gate, raised slogans, and began burning tires at the gate demonstrating. After some in the crowd began throwing stones, we tried to persuade them. The crowd of demonstrators kept coming from various locations and lanes. When we arrived at our office, demonstrators were already vandalizing the office. Commanders tried to persuade the crowd, but they became even more aggressive and the entire office had already been completely vandalized. Demonstrators repeatedly threw petrol bombs while some used fire torches on long batons to set fire inside the office from outside the wall. Many times we extinguished the fires with water from jars and fire extinguishers. As they also threw fire at vehicles from outside, we extinguished those fires many times. After that, we fired aerial fire and tear gas, dispersed the demonstrators, and though the office was vandalized, we were preventing arson. This process of protecting the office kept repeating many times. Demonstrators threw stones and set fires from all four sides. A petrol bomb thrown by demonstrators hit the office chief's body, and he was also hit by stones and seriously injured. As the crowd of demonstrators kept growing, we were making aerial fire and blank round fire to try to disperse them. As this repeated many times, the situation began going beyond control. Thousands of demonstrators pelted stones and set fire to the second floor of the barracks from behind; gradually from the front they set fire through the detention room ventilation window. As smoke started coming from the detention cell and arson began at various locations of the office, demonstrators entered the office and tried to loot materials. We were trying to stop them. Finally, carrying as many weapons and equipment as possible, firing aerial shots with blank rounds and tear gas while dispersing the crowd, we went and stayed at the Nepali Army barracks at Nepaltar. During stone-throwing and arson at Police Circle Balaju, 2 demonstrators were injured and taken for treatment, and I heard through social media that they died during treatment. I don't know how or why they died.
107. Statement of Police Constable Kshetraraj Timalsena, serving at Police Circle Swayambhu:

On Bhadra 23, 2082, since I was serving at the Supply Branch, I was on standby at the office that day. I learned about the events of that day after the duty officer informed me and news came through social media. On Bhadra 24, 2082, a police team went from this office from the morning  under DSP's command to New Baneswhar, under Police Inspector Janak G's command to Kalankhi Chowk, and under Police Inspector Dinesh Thapa's command to the CDO Office junction. For office security, there were 30–35 personnel including branch staff under Police Inspector Suraj Bania's command. At approximately 11:30 AM that day, upon receiving information that VIP residences in this office's ward area had been vandalized and set on fire, a team of 10 under a junior officer's command was deployed to Sitapaila Chowk. Per higher office orders, beat police also returned to this office at 12:00 noon. At approximately 12:15 PM, some people first came and threw stones at police and attacked, and police along with some locals tried to stop the attack through persuasion. As the group of demonstrators gradually grew and the situation became more aggressive and terrifying  some individuals carrying rods and khukuris, some seen carrying batons, stones, chunks of concrete, and petrol-filled bottles  the Police Inspector repeatedly made public address announcements requesting and persuading demonstrators, but demonstrators disregarded this and continued attacking continuously. As police and the office came under attack, following the commander's legal orders  graduated baton charge, tear gas, shotgun rubber cartridges, and aerial fire as needed  office security was maintained. Every effort was made to control the continuous attacks from groups of demonstrators. As demonstrators pelted stones from all four sides and broke through walls and the gate, 10–15 police were also injured. As they entered from the south side and began vandalism and arson at vehicles and the barracks, at approximately 14:30, as tear gas shells were running out and the crowd could not be contained without firing live rounds, the Police Inspector ordered 'do not fire live rounds  people's lives are more important than the office.' With the aim of preventing human casualties, we took the weapons and important materials and went to a safe location. When trying to take case files and weapons in a one-ton vehicle, it was not possible to get out through the gate, so we took as many weapons as we could carry and went to the nearby Military Hospital Chhauni. On that day as well, from our team, per legal orders, only tear gas shells for crowd control, shotgun rubber cartridges, and live bullets for aerial fire were used, and no demonstrators were injured and no human casualties occurred. This is true and accurate. Police personnel were injured from demonstrators' attacks and are still receiving treatment. The office building, vehicles, weapons, case files, supply materials, and personal clothing and belongings of police personnel were all completely burned and destroyed by demonstrators' vandalism and arson. This is true and accurate.
108. Statement of Police Constable Hikmat B.K., serving at Federal Parliament Building Security Base, Baneswhar:

High Point Posts are maintained on all four sides of the Federal Parliament Building, and personnel are deployed at those posts around the clock on rotating duty with INSAS rifles. That area is a restricted zone where activities such as sit-ins, processions, and demonstrations are prohibited. Parliament sessions are held at the Federal Parliament Building, and additional security personnel are deployed when sessions are in progress. On Bhadra 23, 2082, my duty was at High Post No. 22 on the south side of the Federal Parliament Building from 10:00 AM to 14:00 with an INSAS rifle and 36 rounds of ammunition. As the National Assembly session was scheduled to be held that day, additional security from Nepal Police and Armed Police Force had also been deployed. I was on my own duty. At approximately 11:00 to 12:00, thousands of demonstrators from all four sides, growing agitated, surrounded the Parliament Building and were demonstrating. While demonstrating, demonstrators suddenly became agitated and in an anarchic manner began breaking through the Parliament Building compound walls and vandalizing and setting fire to police posts. Immediately, additional support from Nepal Police and Armed Police Force arrived and began persuading the demonstrators not to engage in unruly activities like vandalism and arson, requesting them to demonstrate peacefully. Demonstrators did not listen to police and became more anarchic, throwing stones and injuring police while attempting to break the Parliament Building and enter, and came near my post and began throwing stones at me as well. I came out from my post with my weapon and fell back somewhat. As demonstrators became even more aggressive, sounds of gas gun and bullet fire began to be heard. I did not fire my weapon. In the evening, the crowd went to their respective destinations and a calm atmosphere prevailed. I learned through Facebook and various social media that demonstrators had been injured and had also died. That evening, a Nepali Army team also came and began providing security for the Federal Parliament Building. On the following day, the 24th, from the morning, Nepal Army, Armed Police Force, Nepal Police, and police personnel from the Federal Parliament Building Security Base Baneswhar were all providing security for the Federal Parliament Building. That day I was in crowd control attire and had a baton. That day too, the Gen-Z generation was conducting an all-around movement. A large group of demonstrators had surrounded the Parliament Building from all four sides and was demonstrating. While security police were continuing to persuade demonstrators, at approximately 13:00, an uncontrollable large number gathered around the Parliament Building and targeted it with stone-throwing, slingshots, and petrol bombs and other flammable substances  injuring many police  broke through the Parliament Building compound walls using pickaxes, crowbars, and other tools, and entered the Parliament Building compound brandishing khukuris, spears, and batons, setting fire to the Parliament Building. In that situation, no protective response was made by the Nepali Army. After that, we secured our weapons and ammunition.
109. Statement of Police Constable Chuman Singh Dhami, serving at Police Circle Thankot:

During demonstrations by the Gen-Z youth generation in Kathmandu and various parts of the country on Bhadra 23 and 24, 2082, on Bhadra 23 I was deployed at Maitighar Mandala at approximately 10:00 AM under the command of Police Circle Thankot's DSP Eliza Giri with a team of 15 personnel, and on Bhadra 24 I was deployed for Police Circle Thankot security from 4:00 AM under DSP Eliza Giri's command with a team of 35 personnel for office security  other police personnel from the office were on regular mobile and other duties, and a police team under 2 Police Inspectors was deployed as additional support outside the office. On Bhadra 23, under DSP Eliza Giri's command with a team of 15 personnel, I had taken duty in full gear at Maitighar Mandala at approximately 10:00 AM. At that time, approximately 1,000 people had gathered at Maitighar, and some of those gathered were in school and college uniforms. Those gathered were raising slogans that demonstrations would be peaceful and non-disruptive. After that, the number of people gathering at Maitighar Mandala kept increasing and must have reached 10,000–12,000. While gathering, various individuals were making speeches, and we were on duty in positions to cordon off the crowd. Between approximately 11:30 and 12:00, the crowd that had gathered at Maitighar Mandala walked toward New Baneswhar, and some people were also riding in an open pickup truck making announcements. The crowd moving forward was getting more agitated and it was quite difficult for our team including myself to cordon it off while keeping pace. Most of the people present in the crowd were in the youth age group and they were not prepared to listen to our requests. As we advanced, when the crowd reached the Road Department area, it began moving forward while blocking the entire road and not allowing vehicle traffic. As we advanced further and the crowd reached the Thapagaun bridge, when our team tried to take it via only the left-side road while cordoning it, the crowd became further enraged, verbally abusing police with obscene words while taking up the entire road and advancing. After that, the crowd reached the barricade in front of Everest Hotel where there was also an additional police team, and up until reaching there our team including myself had been cordoning the crowd from the front. After that, our team was behind the barricade while the crowd was raising abusive slogans and chanting more aggressively. By that time, approximately 15,000 people must have gathered. After that, the crowd began trying to break the barricade, throwing bottles, and tearing down the banner marking the restricted zone. During this, even after our team including myself persuaded the crowd, it was not ready to listen, and even when volunteers present there persuaded them, the crowd's agitation and aggression only grew further. Some time later, approximately 50–100 motorcycles in a rally came down from the TR overpass and merged with the crowd. After that, the crowd became even more agitated and aggressive. During this, while we tried hard to prevent the crowd from breaking the barricade, the crowd broke through and advanced toward the Parliament Building. Water cannon from Nepal Police was also used to spray water to stop the crowd. After the crowd broke the barricade and advanced toward the Parliament Building, we took duty outside the Citizen Investment Fund gate side of the Parliament Building as per the commander's orders. After that, the crowd began throwing stones at police and breaking Parliament Building walls. During this, the crowd threw excessive stones at our team including myself, and many police colleagues including my commander and myself had been injured. As they attempted to enter the Parliament Building through the gate where we were on duty, we tried to baton-charge below the knee to disperse the crowd. After many of us including myself had been heavily injured, we went inside the Parliament Building for approximately 45 minutes for primary treatment and after treatment resumed duty at the same position. They were also burning tires. During this, an Armed Police Force ambulance that came to rescue injured police personnel was also set on fire by some members of that crowd. After that, there was a confrontation between agitators and us for some time during which we used baton charges, gas guns, and rubber bullets. During this, 5 dummy rounds were used per commander's orders but we did not use live rounds. After that, how many rounds were fired from other teams and how many were injured  I could not determine anything about this. After that, the crowd continued breaking Parliament Building walls, climbing walls, and throwing stones at police. As the crowd near the Parliament Building thinned and congestion increased toward Everest Hospital, our team took duty toward Everest Hospital. Even there, for some time agitators continued throwing stones at us. After being deployed at that location until approximately 21:30, we returned to our own office per orders, and on the way back we also received treatment at Everest Hospital.

On Bhadra 24, I was deployed for Police Circle Thankot security from 4:00 AM under DSP Eliza Giri's command with a team of 35 personnel for office security. At approximately 11:00 AM, approximately 3,000–4,000 people had already gathered in front of our office building. After that, the crowd was only engaging in activities such as slogan-chanting and burning tires. After that, the crowd headed toward Satungal. At approximately 15:00, approximately 4,000–5,000 people gathered again in front of our office building and began raising slogans. After that, some unruly individuals in the crowd began throwing stones and petrol bombs at us and our office building. While persuading and containing as much as possible, when persuasion was no longer working, we worked to contain and stop them as much as possible. At approximately 16:30, when the crowd was about to enter the office, we chased them outside. After that, per the commander's orders, while pushing through that crowd, we safely transported all office vehicles except 2 motorcycles, weapons, and ammunition, and went to stay at the Nepali Army's training institute at the Godaam. That day too, some of our police personnel were injured.
110. Statement of Police Constable Bakhat Giri, serving at Police Circle Singha Durbar, Kathmandu:

While demonstrations were happening in various parts of the country, on Bhadra 23 I was deployed on duty from approximately 9:30 AM under the command of this office's Chief DSP Janardhana Ghimire at Maitighar Mandala.

At that time, approximately 4,000 youth were present raising various slogans against the then government. Some time later, the crowd advanced toward Baneswhar while we stayed at Maitighar on duty without going to Baneswhar. Although most people had advanced toward Baneswhar, there was still a notable presence of demonstrators at Maitighar. Upon seeing ambulances coming and going and being informed by my commander as well, I learned that some demonstrators had been injured by police bullets in Baneswhar. While at Maitighar Mandala, after my commander said that even the District Government Attorney Office located there could be attacked, our team was deployed for the District Government Attorney Office's security. During this, in the evening we used force up to baton charges to disperse the crowd gathered there. During this, our team did not use tear gas, rubber bullets, or live rounds. At approximately 20:30, after the crowd gathering and demonstrations stopped, we returned to the office and slept in the barracks per the commander's orders.

On Bhadra 24, 2082, at approximately 8:00 AM, I along with my commander DSP Janardhana Ghimire's team reached Maitighar Mandala. At that time, some people were present at Maitighar but not many. Per orders, when the DSP's team went toward Bhadrakali, I stayed behind in the vehicle for the DSP's vehicle security and went inside through Singha Durbar's western gate. While there, at approximately 10:30 AM, a crowd of approximately 7,000 carrying spears, shovels, rods, and other improvised weapons broke through Singha Durbar's western gate and entered Singha Durbar. From there, I (and the vehicle's driver) went to the police security unit inside Singha Durbar to protect the vehicle and kept it safe there.

While the weapons at the police security unit inside Singha Durbar were being moved to the Nepali Army's office, I handed over the SLR and ammunition I had with me to the Nepali Army's office. After that, we went and sheltered in the barracks at the Nepali Army's eastern gate. At approximately 10:00 PM, after meeting with my commander again, I came back to my office Singha Durbar in the vehicle. When we arrived at the office, our office building was burning and some individuals were attempting to steal materials from our office. Of those, we apprehended 4 males and handed them over to District Police Range Kathmandu. After that, at approximately 1:00 AM, we along with the DSP went and slept inside the Nepali Army's Jangi Adda.
5.1.5 Statements and Incident Description Documents of Armed Police Force Nepal:
111. Statement of Armed Police Inspector General Raju Aryal:

Regular reporting to the organization chief to obtain information about the actual situation of any movement is done through the head of the Armed Police Force Headquarters Operations Department. Reporting and directive chains among the AIGP, DIGP, SSP, and SP levels have been maintained in accordance with the chain of command.

Prior to the movement on Bhadra 23, 2082, no reporting had been done through the Armed Police Force's intelligence gathering system and organizational structure about the social media platforms such as Reddit, Discord, and X being used by the Gen-Z youth generation. Although no concrete prior intelligence was received about plans to attack the Federal Parliament Building from the movement, based on analysis, following the movement and events at Tinkune on Chaitra 15 of the previous year, the Baneswhar area where the Federal Parliament Building is located had been designated as a high security challenge area. Instructions had been given through the Operations Department according to organizational structure to Armed Police Force Nepal commanders to analyze security challenges, adopt security alertness, formulate integrated security plans from all district security committees, and maintain effective coordination with other security agencies for security deployment. Prior to the Government of Nepal's decision to shut down social media, no opinion or advice had been sought from Armed Police Force Nepal regarding the security challenges that could arise from it.

During the course of the Bhadra 23 movement, while at the Home Ministry, he received information through the Operations Department that agitators were breaking through the restricted zone and coming toward the Federal Parliament Building. After assessing the situation, he gave directives through the Operations Department head for effective and coordinated security management. While still at the Home Ministry, he received information that demonstrators had broken through the security cordon and entered the Federal Parliament Building premises, and all security agency heads gave directives to their respective agencies to operate effectively and in a coordinated manner. As the situation became increasingly abnormal, after the curfew order issued by the District Administration Office Kathmandu, instructions were given to operate effectively in a coordinated manner with the District Security Committee / Chief District Officer and all security agencies according to the provisions of the Local Administration Act, following organizational structure.

Even as demonstrators threw stones, beat, and attacked security personnel and injured them, he had given instructions to the officers under him to fulfill their responsibilities in a restrained yet effective manner. Regarding coordination with the Nepali Army, he states that coordination was happening at the Prime Minister, Home Minister, and Home Secretary levels, and that he had maintained necessary information and coordination in his own capacity. He clarifies that the decision to fire was not made by him personally, and that field-deployed security personnel made necessary decisions based on law, SOP, and situational assessment. He claims that he did not receive any official report of lethal shooting at the crowd in violation of international police standards and human rights norms by the Armed Police Force, but claims aerial firing did occur. He states that all Armed Police Force personnel deployed at the Parliament Building premises were familiar with crowd control SOPs and that a Layered Defense Plan was also being implemented.

He clarifies that Armed Police Force Nepal was deployed as part of the integrated security plan per the District Security Committee's decision, not as a dedicated security force for the Federal Parliament Building, but was deployed to handle the immediate situation. According to him, field commanders kept the principle of 'life over property' at the center and adopted maximum restraint.

After the Bhadra 23 Federal Parliament Building incident, a security briefing for security chiefs was held with the Prime Minister. Discussing potential security challenges that could arise in Kathmandu and across the country from the second day, instructions were given to subordinate units to maintain high alertness, deploy necessary forces, protect and promote human rights, prevent further human casualties, adopt 'life over property,' keep contingency plans ready. Additionally, instructions were given to exercise restraint in use of force, ensure protection of human rights, and adhere to the principle of minimum use of force.

On the morning of Bhadra 24, they arrived at Prime Minister's residence Baluwatar. After informing the Prime Minister about crowd control and security challenges, arson, vandalism, and looting at public places, the Prime Minister acknowledged the seriousness of the incident and gave directives to bring greater effectiveness to coordination among security agencies.
He described the deadly attacks on security personnel and killings at various locations on Bhadra 24 as extremely condemnable and as a serious challenge to the state's security structure.

Regarding damage, looting of weapons and ammunition during security management, he states that a joint coordinated action plan among Armed Police Force Nepal, Nepal Police, Nepali Army, and district administration was immediately implemented, and search and recovery of looted weapons, ammunition, and prisoners who escaped from prisons is ongoing.

He confirmed that after the security briefing at Baluwatar on Bhadra 24, he along with other security agency chiefs arrived at the Jangi Adda via Nepali Army helicopter, discussed security management and coordination there, and returned to Armed Police Force Nepal Headquarters that same evening.
He states that the authority to order lethal force does not fall within his jurisdiction under the Local Administration Act and that he did not give any such order.

No Armed Police Force personnel died during the Bhadra 23 and 24 movement, but some were injured, and personal vehicles of Armed Police Force personnel, office supplies, and security equipment were also damaged or looted. In that movement, SLR 10 barrels, gas gun 4 barrels, and related ammunition went missing from Armed Police Force No. 1 Baraha Battalion Headquarters Sunsari; and SLR 11 barrels, gas gun 2 barrels, SMG 1 barrel, and related ammunition went missing from Armed Police Force No. 9 Pashupati Battalion Headquarters Kathmandu  reports to this effect have been received.

A list of security personnel who performed their duties courageously has been prepared and arrangements have been made to award them on Armed Police Day. An internal investigation committee has been formed for those who evaded responsibilities and showed weakness  after the report comes, necessary legal/disciplinary action will be taken.

He states that Armed Police Force Nepal security personnel operated professionally despite limited resources and manpower. In the course of maintaining professionalism, adverse circumstances were created. He states that the Armed Police Force did not fail in professionalism at any point.

In the latter part of his statement, he emphasizes that to prevent recurrence of such events in the future, administrative reform, human resource capacity development, regular training, adequate practice of non-lethal measures, sensitivity toward human rights protection, and preparedness are essential. He notes that to maintain high security personnel morale, career security, compensation, insurance, and social security assurance are necessary. He concludes that a neutral and professional security mechanism free from political interference is the long-term solution. Overall, Raju Aryal's statement presents the viewpoint that the events were not the result of intentional or standards-violating actions by the Armed Police Force, but rather the outcome of security management conducted within legal bounds under extremely complex and pressured circumstances.
112. Statement of Armed Police Additional Inspector General Narayandatta Paudel:

The initial intelligence provided by subordinate battalions about the demonstration organized by the Gen-Z group was that it was estimated to remain limited peacefully within the Mandala-New Baneswhar perimeter. The security plan too was based on this estimate, with the integrated security plan formed by the district security committee, and subordinate battalions deployed accordingly. As no additional prior intelligence or social media analysis (Reddit, Discord, X, etc.) had been received from the Armed Police side, he had not received signals of high threat up until the previous day.

As crowds rapidly grew in the valley from the morning of Bhadra 23, he mentions being 'limited to administrative coordination' in his capacity as departmental head. He mentions that the Armed Police Force's ambulance being set on fire and the curfew being declared in Kathmandu was known through battalion reports. He mentions that he had previously given instructions to Armed Police officers deployed in the field for minimum use of force, protection of human rights, and continuous coordination with local administration. He claims that even after ambulance arson, stone-throwing and beating of security personnel, and attempts to push toward the inner security cordon began, the report he received was that only aerial fire had been done from the Armed Police side.

Regarding the deadly shooting involving injuries to chest, mouth, head, or throat seen in post-mortem reports of deceased demonstrators  with only 19 rounds of aerial fire reported from the Armed Police, their deaths would not involve any role from the Armed Police.

On the following day, Bhadra 24, as the situation became abnormal across the country, he states he remained at headquarters focused on debriefing with unit chiefs across the country, alertness orders, and contingency planning. As arson, vandalism, and looting began at police offices, party offices, government structures, and commercial establishments across many places in the valley and the country, he states he gave instructions to subordinates for high alertness, cooperation with other security agencies, priority protection of key structures, immediate hospitalization of the injured, and use of minimum  yet effective  force.

On Bhadra 24 in the valley, the Armed Police Force Nepal Security Base Min Bhawan Kathmandu and the Bhutanese Refugee Security Base at Beldangi in Jhapa district suffered weapon and equipment damage through arson and looting. He estimates the events of that day occurred due to 'an abnormally large, leaderless, and violent crowd and attacks that began suddenly.' According to him, the low number of security personnel in the valley, weak physical structures, and most deployed field manpower being busy at other important locations created challenges in controlling looting and arson. However, he notes that no deaths from the Armed Police side were reported anywhere in the country on Bhadra 24.

On the issue of infiltration in the movement, he clearly presents the conclusion that outside groups entered the peaceful crowd and spread frenzy. His assessment is that the movement became violent and destructive precisely because of the aggressive activities of these infiltrating groups.

The main causes of the events of Bhadra 23–24 were: the creation of a large, disorganized, and leaderless crowd due to various reasons; dissatisfied parties not being addressed in time; shortfall in prior intelligence about crowd size and possible events; insufficient monitoring of activities on social media such as Reddit, Discord, and X; risk to sensitive structures; aggressive and destructive conduct; and participation of infiltrating groups. To prevent such events in the future: strengthening of preparedness, intelligence systems, and mechanisms; timely formulation of integrated Layered Security Plans; trained security personnel; adequate non-lethal measures; social media monitoring; joint training, exercises, and coordination among security agencies should be further enhanced; and policy reforms to bring clarity to the operational policies adopted by the Armed Police Force and Nepal Police to ensure security against such events in the future are also necessary. Additionally, adequacy of non-lethal equipment, crowd control technology and equipment, weapons, and necessary security materials must be ensured; and security exercises, Layered Defense plans, social media monitoring, and coordination and information systems must be improved  appropriate advice and suggestions are needed, he states.

To make Armed Police Force Nepal strong and professional, he sees the need for: availability of additional manpower to fulfill legally mandated roles; abundance of resources; additional deployment in Kathmandu Valley and major cities; strengthening of border security; state prioritization of information technology use; regular modern training; clarity in legal and operational policies; effective coordination with other security agencies; regular training and integrated exercises and evaluations. To prevent such movements from recurring in the future, priority must be given to institutional reforms such as youth development-oriented prosperity policies; strengthening state institutions based on policy, law, and systems; participatory democracy; transparent decision-making processes; effective grievance management; youth representation at local levels; corruption control and good governance; technology-friendly rapid service delivery; and expansion of education and employment opportunities  this is his conclusion.
